content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:42ec0761

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript includes durable personal/project-device facts and decisions (Backblaze choice, owned spare screwdriver, Anker organizer choice) and a reusable troubleshooting plan for the user's wireless mouse; forced memory write requires non-noop.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:42ec0761:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user chose Google Drive as the cloud backup service for backing up their working files from their laptop.
- The user chose the Google Drive 100 GB plan for backing up their working files.
- The user owns or uses a laptop involved in their backup setup and planned RAM upgrade.
- The user owns a small screwdriver used for opening their laptop, but had misplaced it as of the backup setup conversation.
- The user intended to upgrade the RAM in their laptop.
Open commitments:
- OPEN: Pick up a small screwdriver for laptop RAM upgrade.
- note hot-path-0a69a9e42455-note-2: CONTEXT; Google Drive backup setup approach for working files; The user set up Google Drive as a cloud backup system for working files, choosing the 100 GB plan and focusing on folder selection, versioning, backup-status checks, notifications, and periodic monitoring of storage usage/status.; scope=Applies to the user's Google Drive backup setup for working files on the user's laptop.
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-4: prefers_storage; The user chose Google Drive as the cloud backup service for backing up their working files from their laptop.; scope=User's backup setup for working files on their laptop
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-5: prefers_storage; The user chose the Google Drive 100 GB plan for backing up their working files.; scope=User's Google Drive backup storage plan for working files
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-7: owns; The user owns a small screwdriver used for opening their laptop, but had misplaced it as of the backup setup conversation.; scope=User's small laptop screwdriver for laptop RAM upgrade work
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-6: owns; The user owns or uses a laptop involved in their backup setup and planned RAM upgrade.; scope=User's laptop referenced in backup setup and RAM upgrade context
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-8: pending_pickup; The user needs to pick up a small screwdriver for a planned laptop RAM upgrade, likely from the electronics store near their place.; scope=Pending errand for the user's laptop RAM upgrade tool
- claim hot-path-0a69a9e42455-claim-9: has_goal; The user intended to upgrade the RAM in their laptop.; scope=User's laptop maintenance plan
- note hot-path-0a69a9e42455-note-3: PLAN; Planned screwdriver pickup for laptop RAM upgrade; The user intends to open the laptop for a RAM upgrade but misplaced the small screwdriver used for that task; the likely acquisition plan is to check the electronics store near the user's place for a replacement screwdriver.; scope=Applies to the user's laptop RAM-upgrade preparation and tool acquisition plan.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=my working files; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=my working files; reason=Candidate existing entity matches the user's working files.
- mention=Backblaze; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=SERVICE; name=Backblaze; reason=Backblaze is a concrete backup service chosen or discussed by the user and no existing candidate matches it.

- mention=Carbonite; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=SERVICE; name=Carbonite; reason=Concrete backup service option discussed; no existing candidate matches.

- mention=Google One; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=SERVICE; name=Google One; reason=Mentioned as Google Drive subscription context; concrete service absent from candidates.
- mention=my laptop; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<12>; type=existing; name=my laptop; reason=Candidate existing entity matches the user's laptop.
- mention=spare screwdriver; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's spare screwdriver; reason=The user states they have a spare screwdriver picked up while organizing their desk. It may differ from the existing small laptop screwdriver, which was previously misplaced, so avoid merging on weak evidence.
- mention=the small screwdriver I use for that; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<14>; type=existing; name=the small screwdriver I use for that; reason=Retrieval context references the existing small laptop screwdriver used for opening the laptop; current mention of screwdriver for opening laptop is related, though the explicitly spare screwdriver is kept distinct.
- mention=my computer desk; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's computer desk; reason=Concrete personal item mentioned as the place or context where the spare screwdriver was found; no existing candidate matches.
- mention=Anker Cable Organizer Bag; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=PRODUCT; name=Anker Cable Organizer Bag; reason=Concrete product recommendation later chosen by the user; no existing candidate matches.
- mention=the Anker one; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=PRODUCT; name=Anker Cable Organizer Bag; reason=This refers to the Anker Cable Organizer Bag. Since no existing entity id is available in candidates, create the same canonical identity rather than using add_alias without an id.
- mention=my bag; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<17>; type=PRODUCT; name=User's computer bag; reason=Concrete personal item where the cable organizer will fit; no existing candidate matches.

Session date: 2023/08/15 (Tue) 13:26
Transcript:
user: I need help setting up automatic backups for my working files. What are some good options for backup software?
assistant: Setting up automatic backups is a crucial step in protecting your important files! There are many excellent backup software options available, and I'll outline some popular ones for you:

1. **Backblaze**: A popular and affordable cloud-based backup service that automatically backs up your files to the cloud. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
2. **Acronis True Image**: A comprehensive backup solution that offers local and cloud backup options, as well as disk imaging and ransomware protection. (Windows, Mac)
3. **Carbonite**: A cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac)
4. **CrashPlan**: A popular cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
5. **Time Machine** (Mac): A built-in backup solution for Macs that automatically backs up your files to an external hard drive.
6. **Windows Backup** (Windows): A built-in backup solution for Windows that allows you to create system image backups and file backups to an external hard drive or network location.
7. **Duplicati**: A free, open-source backup software that supports local and cloud backup options, with encryption and versioning. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
8. **Areca Backup**: A free, open-source backup software that supports local and cloud backup options, with encryption and versioning. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
9. **IDrive**: A cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac)
10. **Google Drive** (Google One): A cloud storage service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for syncing and sharing. (Web, Android, iOS)

When choosing a backup software, consider the following factors:

* **Ease of use**: How easy is the software to set up and use?
* **Automatic backup**: Does the software offer automatic backup options?
* **Storage capacity**: How much storage space do you need, and what's the cost?
* **Security**: Does the software offer encryption and secure data transfer?
* **Compatibility**: Is the software compatible with your operating system and devices?
* **Features**: Do you need additional features like versioning, syncing, or disk imaging?

Before selecting a backup software, you may also want to consider the 3-2-1 backup rule:

1. **Three** copies of your data: One primary copy (your working files) and two backup copies.
2. **Two** different storage types: One local backup (e.g., external hard drive) and one offsite backup (e.g., cloud storage).
3. **One** offsite copy: Store one backup copy in a different location, such as the cloud or an external hard drive at a different location.

I hope this helps you get started with setting up automatic backups for your working files!
user: I'm leaning towards Backblaze, but how does it handle file versioning? I also need to organize my computer bag, do you have any recommendations for cable organizers?
assistant: **Backblaze File Versioning:**

Backblaze offers a robust file versioning system, which allows you to recover previous versions of your files in case you need to. Here's how it works:

* **Version history**: Backblaze keeps a version history of your files for up to 30 days. This means that if you need to recover a file from a specific point in time, you can do so within that 30-day window.
* **File versions**: Backblaze stores multiple versions of your files, allowing you to recover previous versions in case you need to. The number of versions stored depends on the file type and the frequency of changes.
* **Restore options**: You can restore individual files or entire folders to a specific point in time using the Backblaze web interface or the desktop application.

**Cable Organizers:**

A well-organized computer bag can make a huge difference in productivity and stress levels. Here are some excellent cable organizer recommendations:

1. **CableOrganizer.com Cable Ties**: These reusable cable ties are perfect for keeping cables tidy and organized. They come in various sizes and are easy to use.
2. **Anker Cable Organizer Bag**: This compact bag is designed specifically for cables and features multiple compartments and pockets to keep everything organized.
3. **Bellroy CableOrganizer**: This premium cable organizer features a sleek design and multiple compartments to store cables, adapters, and other accessories.
4. **TravelWise Cable Organizer**: This compact organizer features a zippered pouch and multiple compartments to store cables, earbuds, and other small accessories.
5. **CordConqueror Cable Organizer**: This innovative organizer features a unique design that allows you to wrap cables around a central hub, keeping them tidy and organized.

When choosing a cable organizer, consider the following factors:

* **Size**: How much space do you have available in your computer bag?
* **Compartments**: Do you need multiple compartments to separate different types of cables or accessories?
* **Material**: Do you prefer a soft, fabric-based organizer or a harder, more rigid one?
* **Portability**: How often do you need to take your cable organizer on the go?

I hope this helps you make an informed decision about Backblaze's file versioning and find the perfect cable organizer for your needs!
user: I think I'll go with Backblaze, thanks for the info. By the way, I need to open up my laptop to clean the fans soon, do I have a spare screwdriver for that?
assistant: **Backblaze is a great choice!**

**Laptop Screwdriver:**

When it comes to opening up your laptop, you'll likely need a specialized screwdriver called a Torx driver or a Pentalobe driver, depending on your laptop model. These drivers are designed to fit the unique screws used in laptops.

Here's what you can do:

1. **Check your laptop's documentation**: Look for the user manual or online documentation that came with your laptop. It might specify the type of screwdriver required for maintenance and repairs.
2. **Check online forums**: Search for forums or communities dedicated to your laptop model. You can ask for advice on the type of screwdriver needed and any specific tips for cleaning the fans.
3. **Purchase a laptop screwdriver set**: You can buy a set of screwdrivers specifically designed for laptops. These sets usually include a variety of Torx and Pentalobe drivers in different sizes.
4. **Borrow from a friend or local electronics store**: If you have a friend who's handy with electronics or a local electronics store that offers repair services, you might be able to borrow the necessary screwdriver.

Some popular options for laptop screwdriver sets include:

* iFixit's Laptop Screwdriver Set
* AmazonBasics Laptop Screwdriver Set
* Wiha Tools Laptop Screwdriver Set

Remember to handle the internal components of your laptop with care, and consider consulting online tutorials or guides specific to your laptop model before attempting to clean the fans.

Stay safe and happy cleaning!
user: I actually have a spare screwdriver that I picked up when I organized my computer desk a while back, so I'm all set there. Now, about that cable organizer... I think I'll go with the Anker one, it seems like it'll fit nicely in my bag.
assistant: **Spare screwdriver**: Ah, excellent! You're all set for cleaning those laptop fans then!

**Anker Cable Organizer**: Awesome choice! The Anker Cable Organizer is a popular and highly-rated option for a reason. Its compact design and multiple compartments will help keep your cables and accessories tidy and organized, making it easy to find what you need when you need it.

The Anker Cable Organizer is also a great fit for most laptop bags and backpacks, so you can rest assured it'll fit nicely in your bag. Plus, it's made of durable materials and has a sleek design, making it a great addition to your tech accessories.

**Tips for using your new cable organizer:**

1. **Categorize your cables**: Separate your cables into categories, such as charging cables, audio cables, and USB cables, to make it easier to find what you need.
2. **Use the compartments wisely**: Make the most of the different compartments in your cable organizer by assigning specific cables or accessories to each one.
3. **Keep it tidy**: Try to keep your cable organizer tidy by wrapping cables neatly and storing them in their designated compartments.
4. **Consider labeling**: If you have multiple cables that look similar, consider labeling them with small stickers or tags to make it easier to identify them.

With your new cable organizer, you'll be able to find what you need quickly, reduce clutter, and make your travels more efficient.

Enjoy your new cable organizer, and happy organizing!
user: What's the best way to troubleshoot my wireless mouse's freezing issue? Should I try resetting the mouse or updating the drivers?
assistant: **Troubleshooting a wireless mouse's freezing issue:**

To troubleshoot your wireless mouse's freezing issue, follow these steps:

1. **Basic Troubleshooting**:
	* Restart your computer and mouse: Sometimes, a simple reboot can resolve the issue.
	* Check the battery level: Ensure the mouse's batteries are not low. Try replacing them with new ones or charging the mouse if it's rechargeable.
	* Move the mouse closer to the receiver: Interference from other devices can cause connectivity issues. Try moving the mouse closer to the receiver.
2. **Interference Troubleshooting**:
	* Identify potential sources of interference: Other wireless devices, cordless phones, and microwaves can cause interference. Try moving these devices away from the mouse receiver.
	* Change the receiver's location: Move the receiver to a different location to see if the issue persists.
3. **Driver Updates**:
	* Check for driver updates: Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues. Go to the manufacturer's website and check for updates.
	* Update the drivers: Install the latest drivers and see if the issue resolves.
4. **Reset the Mouse**:
	* Check the manufacturer's instructions: Look for specific reset instructions in the user manual or online documentation.
	* Reset the mouse: Follow the instructions to reset the mouse to its factory settings.
5. **Advanced Troubleshooting**:
	* Use the built-in troubleshooting tools: Some mice have built-in troubleshooting tools or software that can help diagnose the issue.
	* Try a different receiver or mouse: If you have a spare receiver or mouse, try swapping them to isolate the issue.

**Resetting the mouse vs. updating drivers:**

If you've tried the basic troubleshooting steps and the issue persists, I'd recommend updating the drivers first. Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues, and updating them might resolve the problem.

If updating the drivers doesn't work, then try resetting the mouse. Resetting the mouse will restore its factory settings, which can sometimes resolve issues caused by configuration or software problems.

Remember to follow the manufacturer's instructions for resetting the mouse, as the process may vary depending on the model.

I hope this helps you troubleshoot and resolve your wireless mouse's freezing issue!
user: Let me try to reset the mouse using the manufacturer's instructions. If that doesn't work, I'll update the drivers and see if that resolves the issue.
assistant: **Resetting the Mouse:**

Excellent approach! Following the manufacturer's instructions to reset the mouse is a great next step. This will help you determine if the issue is related to the mouse's configuration or software.

Make sure to follow the instructions carefully and thoroughly to ensure the reset process is completed successfully. If you're unsure about any part of the process, you can always refer to the user manual or contact the manufacturer's support team for assistance.

**Update Drivers as a Next Step:**

If resetting the mouse doesn't resolve the issue, updating the drivers is a logical next step. Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues, and updating them may resolve the problem.

Remember to go to the manufacturer's website and download the latest drivers for your specific mouse model. Follow the installation instructions carefully, and make sure to restart your computer after updating the drivers.

**Additional Tips:**

* If you're using a wireless mouse with a receiver, try unplugging the receiver and plugging it back in after resetting the mouse or updating the drivers.
* If you're using a Bluetooth mouse, try disconnecting and reconnecting the mouse from your computer's Bluetooth settings.
* If you're still experiencing issues after resetting the mouse and updating the drivers, you may want to consider contacting the manufacturer's support team for further assistance or considering a replacement mouse.

Keep me updated on your progress, and I'll be happy to provide further guidance if needed!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as

- `Task`

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/08/15 (Tue) 13:26
Transcript:
user: I need help setting up automatic backups for my working files. What are some good options for backup software?
assistant: Setting up automatic backups is a crucial step in protecting your important files! There are many excellent backup software options available, and I'll outline some popular ones for you:

1. **Backblaze**: A popular and affordable cloud-based backup service that automatically backs up your files to the cloud. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
2. **Acronis True Image**: A comprehensive backup solution that offers local and cloud backup options, as well as disk imaging and ransomware protection. (Windows, Mac)
3. **Carbonite**: A cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac)
4. **CrashPlan**: A popular cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
5. **Time Machine** (Mac): A built-in backup solution for Macs that automatically backs up your files to an external hard drive.
6. **Windows Backup** (Windows): A built-in backup solution for Windows that allows you to create system image backups and file backups to an external hard drive or network location.
7. **Duplicati**: A free, open-source backup software that supports local and cloud backup options, with encryption and versioning. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
8. **Areca Backup**: A free, open-source backup software that supports local and cloud backup options, with encryption and versioning. (Windows, Mac, Linux)
9. **IDrive**: A cloud-based backup service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for external hard drive backup and syncing. (Windows, Mac)
10. **Google Drive** (Google One): A cloud storage service that offers automatic backup of your files, with options for syncing and sharing. (Web, Android, iOS)

When choosing a backup software, consider the following factors:

* **Ease of use**: How easy is the software to set up and use?
* **Automatic backup**: Does the software offer automatic backup options?
* **Storage capacity**: How much storage space do you need, and what's the cost?
* **Security**: Does the software offer encryption and secure data transfer?
* **Compatibility**: Is the software compatible with your operating system and devices?
* **Features**: Do you need additional features like versioning, syncing, or disk imaging?

Before selecting a backup software, you may also want to consider the 3-2-1 backup rule:

1. **Three** copies of your data: One primary copy (your working files) and two backup copies.
2. **Two** different storage types: One local backup (e.g., external hard drive) and one offsite backup (e.g., cloud storage).
3. **One** offsite copy: Store one backup copy in a different location, such as the cloud or an external hard drive at a different location.

I hope this helps you get started with setting up automatic backups for your working files!
user: I'm leaning towards Backblaze, but how does it handle file versioning? I also need to organize my computer bag, do you have any recommendations for cable organizers?
assistant: **Backblaze File Versioning:**

Backblaze offers a robust file versioning system, which allows you to recover previous versions of your files in case you need to. Here's how it works:

* **Version history**: Backblaze keeps a version history of your files for up to 30 days. This means that if you need to recover a file from a specific point in time, you can do so within that 30-day window.
* **File versions**: Backblaze stores multiple versions of your files, allowing you to recover previous versions in case you need to. The number of versions stored depends on the file type and the frequency of changes.
* **Restore options**: You can restore individual files or entire folders to a specific point in time using the Backblaze web interface or the desktop application.

**Cable Organizers:**

A well-organized computer bag can make a huge difference in productivity and stress levels. Here are some excellent cable organizer recommendations:

1. **CableOrganizer.com Cable Ties**: These reusable cable ties are perfect for keeping cables tidy and organized. They come in various sizes and are easy to use.
2. **Anker Cable Organizer Bag**: This compact bag is designed specifically for cables and features multiple compartments and pockets to keep everything organized.
3. **Bellroy CableOrganizer**: This premium cable organizer features a sleek design and multiple compartments to store cables, adapters, and other accessories.
4. **TravelWise Cable Organizer**: This compact organizer features a zippered pouch and multiple compartments to store cables, earbuds, and other small accessories.
5. **CordConqueror Cable Organizer**: This innovative organizer features a unique design that allows you to wrap cables around a central hub, keeping them tidy and organized.

When choosing a cable organizer, consider the following factors:

* **Size**: How much space do you have available in your computer bag?
* **Compartments**: Do you need multiple compartments to separate different types of cables or accessories?
* **Material**: Do you prefer a soft, fabric-based organizer or a harder, more rigid one?
* **Portability**: How often do you need to take your cable organizer on the go?

I hope this helps you make an informed decision about Backblaze's file versioning and find the perfect cable organizer for your needs!
user: I think I'll go with Backblaze, thanks for the info. By the way, I need to open up my laptop to clean the fans soon, do I have a spare screwdriver for that?
assistant: **Backblaze is a great choice!**

**Laptop Screwdriver:**

When it comes to opening up your laptop, you'll likely need a specialized screwdriver called a Torx driver or a Pentalobe driver, depending on your laptop model. These drivers are designed to fit the unique screws used in laptops.

Here's what you can do:

1. **Check your laptop's documentation**: Look for the user manual or online documentation that came with your laptop. It might specify the type of screwdriver required for maintenance and repairs.
2. **Check online forums**: Search for forums or communities dedicated to your laptop model. You can ask for advice on the type of screwdriver needed and any specific tips for cleaning the fans.
3. **Purchase a laptop screwdriver set**: You can buy a set of screwdrivers specifically designed for laptops. These sets usually include a variety of Torx and Pentalobe drivers in different sizes.
4. **Borrow from a friend or local electronics store**: If you have a friend who's handy with electronics or a local electronics store that offers repair services, you might be able to borrow the necessary screwdriver.

Some popular options for laptop screwdriver sets include:

* iFixit's Laptop Screwdriver Set
* AmazonBasics Laptop Screwdriver Set
* Wiha Tools Laptop Screwdriver Set

Remember to handle the internal components of your laptop with care, and consider consulting online tutorials or guides specific to your laptop model before attempting to clean the fans.

Stay safe and happy cleaning!

assistant: **Spare screwdriver**: Ah, excellent! You're all set for cleaning those laptop fans then!

**Anker Cable Organizer**: Awesome choice! The Anker Cable Organizer is a popular and highly-rated option for a reason. Its compact design and multiple compartments will help keep your cables and accessories tidy and organized, making it easy to find what you need when you need it.

The Anker Cable Organizer is also a great fit for most laptop bags and backpacks, so you can rest assured it'll fit nicely in your bag. Plus, it's made of durable materials and has a sleek design, making it a great addition to your tech accessories.

**Tips for using your new cable organizer:**

1. **Categorize your cables**: Separate your cables into categories, such as charging cables, audio cables, and USB cables, to make it easier to find what you need.
2. **Use the compartments wisely**: Make the most of the different compartments in your cable organizer by assigning specific cables or accessories to each one.
3. **Keep it tidy**: Try to keep your cable organizer tidy by wrapping cables neatly and storing them in their designated compartments.
4. **Consider labeling**: If you have multiple cables that look similar, consider labeling them with small stickers or tags to make it easier to identify them.

With your new cable organizer, you'll be able to find what you need quickly, reduce clutter, and make your travels more efficient.

Enjoy your new cable organizer, and happy organizing!
user: What's the best way to troubleshoot my wireless mouse's freezing issue? Should I try resetting the mouse or updating the drivers?
assistant: **Troubleshooting a wireless mouse's freezing issue:**

To troubleshoot your wireless mouse's freezing issue, follow these steps:

1. **Basic Troubleshooting**:
	* Restart your computer and mouse: Sometimes, a simple reboot can resolve the issue.
	* Check the battery level: Ensure the mouse's batteries are not low. Try replacing them with new ones or charging the mouse if it's rechargeable.
	* Move the mouse closer to the receiver: Interference from other devices can cause connectivity issues. Try moving the mouse closer to the receiver.
2. **Interference Troubleshooting**:
	* Identify potential sources of interference: Other wireless devices, cordless phones, and microwaves can cause interference. Try moving these devices away from the mouse receiver.
	* Change the receiver's location: Move the receiver to a different location to see if the issue persists.
3. **Driver Updates**:
	* Check for driver updates: Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues. Go to the manufacturer's website and check for updates.
	* Update the drivers: Install the latest drivers and see if the issue resolves.
4. **Reset the Mouse**:
	* Check the manufacturer's instructions: Look for specific reset instructions in the user manual or online documentation.
	* Reset the mouse: Follow the instructions to reset the mouse to its factory settings.
5. **Advanced Troubleshooting**:
	* Use the built-in troubleshooting tools: Some mice have built-in troubleshooting tools or software that can help diagnose the issue.
	* Try a different receiver or mouse: If you have a spare receiver or mouse, try swapping them to isolate the issue.

**Resetting the mouse vs. updating drivers:**

If you've tried the basic troubleshooting steps and the issue persists, I'd recommend updating the drivers first. Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues, and updating them might resolve the problem.

If updating the drivers doesn't work, then try resetting the mouse. Resetting the mouse will restore its factory settings, which can sometimes resolve issues caused by configuration or software problems.

Remember to follow the manufacturer's instructions for resetting the mouse, as the process may vary depending on the model.

I hope this helps you troubleshoot and resolve your wireless mouse's freezing issue!
user: Let me try to reset the mouse using the manufacturer's instructions. If that doesn't work, I'll update the drivers and see if that resolves the issue.
assistant: **Resetting the Mouse:**

Excellent approach! Following the manufacturer's instructions to reset the mouse is a great next step. This will help you determine if the issue is related to the mouse's configuration or software.

Make sure to follow the instructions carefully and thoroughly to ensure the reset process is completed successfully. If you're unsure about any part of the process, you can always refer to the user manual or contact the manufacturer's support team for assistance.

**Update Drivers as a Next Step:**

If resetting the mouse doesn't resolve the issue, updating the drivers is a logical next step. Outdated drivers can cause connectivity issues, and updating them may resolve the problem.

Remember to go to the manufacturer's website and download the latest drivers for your specific mouse model. Follow the installation instructions carefully, and make sure to restart your computer after updating the drivers.

**Additional Tips:**

* If you're using a wireless mouse with a receiver, try unplugging the receiver and plugging it back in after resetting the mouse or updating the drivers.
* If you're using a Bluetooth mouse, try disconnecting and reconnecting the mouse from your computer's Bluetooth settings.
* If you're still experiencing issues after resetting the mouse and updating the drivers, you may want to consider contacting the manufacturer's support team for further assistance or considering a replacement mouse.

Keep me updated on your progress, and I'll be happy to provide further guidance if needed!
